title: Input & Conversion
subtitle: Let your programs ASK questions and listen to the answer.
goal: Use input() to talk with the person running your program, and turn their answers into numbers with int() and float() so you can do math with them.

blocks.heading: Programs that talk back 🎤
blocks.html:
So far your programs have done all the talking with print(). Today they learn to listen. The magic word is input() — it stops the program and waits for a human to type something.

In our browser, input() shows a little popup box. You type your answer, press OK, and your program keeps going with whatever you typed. 🪄

blocks.caption: Ask for a name, then say hello
blocks.code:
name = input("What is your name? ")  # type your name in the popup
print("Hello, " + name + "! Nice to meet you. 👋")
blocks.explain: input("...") shows the question in a popup, waits for you, and hands back whatever you typed. We saved it in a box called name so we can use it again.

blocks.html: The popup is your friend! When you press Run and a box appears, that's input() asking you something. Type your answer and press OK to keep going.

blocks.heading: The big secret: input() is ALWAYS text 🧵
blocks.html:
Here's the trick that surprises everyone: no matter what you type, input() gives you back a string (text). Even if you type 7, Python sees the text "7", not the number 7.

That means you can't do math on it yet — adding two text answers just sticks them side by side!

blocks.caption: Watch what goes wrong with text
blocks.code:
a = input("First number: ")   # type 2
b = input("Second number: ")  # type 3
print(a + b)  # surprise! this shows 23, not 5
blocks.explain: Because a and b are TEXT, the + glues them together: "2" + "3" becomes "23". To add them like numbers, we first have to convert them.

blocks.html: Heads up! Text + text = stuck-together text. "2" + "3" is "23", not 5. To do real math, convert first!

blocks.heading: Turning text into numbers with int() and float()
blocks.html:
To fix this we convert the text into a number:

  • int() makes a whole number, like 5 or 42.
  • float() makes a number with a dot, like 3.5 or 9.99.

A super common pattern is to wrap them together: int(input("...")) asks AND converts in one line.

blocks.caption: A tiny calculator that really adds
blocks.code:
a = int(input("First number: "))   # type 2
b = int(input("Second number: "))  # type 3
print(f"{a} + {b} = {a + b}")  # now it really adds! 🎉
blocks.explain: int(input(...)) asks the question AND turns the answer into a whole number. Now a + b does real math. We used an f-string from Day 3 to show it neatly.

blocks.question: What kind of value does input() always give back?
blocks.explain: input() ALWAYS returns a string. Even if you type 7, Python sees the text "7" until you convert it.

blocks.question: If you type 4 and 5 into input() and then print them with +, what shows up?
blocks.explain: Since input() gives text, "4" + "5" glues into "45". You'd need int() to get 9.

blocks.question: Which line asks a question AND turns the answer into a whole number?
blocks.explain: int(input("...")) runs input() first to get the text, then int() turns that text into a whole number.

blocks.title: Friendly greeter
blocks.instructions: Use input() to ask for the person's name, then print a warm hello that includes their name. (When the popup appears, type any name!) Keep the input() in your code.
blocks.starter:
name = input("What is your name? ")  # type your name in the popup
# print a friendly hello that uses name
blocks.solution:
name = input("What is your name? ")
print(f"Hi {name}, welcome to Python! 🐍")
blocks.passMsg: Yay — your program now greets people by name!
blocks.failMsg: Make sure you use input("...") to ask for the name, then print a hello with it.

blocks.title: Double it!
blocks.instructions: Ask the user for a number, convert it with int(), then print that number doubled (the number times 2). Use int(input(...)) so the math works. (Type any number in the popup.)
blocks.starter:
n = int(input("Give me a number: "))  # type a number in the popup
# print n times 2
blocks.solution:
n = int(input("Give me a number: "))
print(f"Double of {n} is {n * 2}!")
blocks.passMsg: Nice! You converted text to a number and did real math. ✨
blocks.failMsg: Use int(input("...")) to get a real number, then print n * 2.

blocks.title: Surprise Quiz! Days 1–5
blocks.intro: Pop quiz! Let's see what stuck from the last 5 days. No grades, just fun 🎉

questions.question: Day 1: What does print("Hi") do?
options: Shows the text Hi on the screen
questions.explain: print() shows whatever is inside the quotes — here, the text Hi — on its own line.

questions.question: Day 2: What type is the value True?
questions.explain: True and False are booleans (bool) — yes/no values. Remember the capital first letter!

questions.question: Day 3: If name = "Sam", what does f"Hi {name}!" print?
questions.explain: The f before the quote tells Python to swap {name} for its value, so you get Hi Sam!

questions.question: Day 4: What does 17 % 5 give you?
questions.explain: % gives the remainder. 5 fits into 17 three times (15), leaving 2 left over.

questions.question: Day 5: What kind of value does input() always give back?
questions.explain: input() always hands back a string. Even if you type 7, it's the text "7" until you convert it with int().

challenge.title: Age in days machine 🎂
challenge.instructions: Build a little program that asks the user how old they are, converts the answer with int(), and then tells them roughly how many DAYS old they are (age times 365). Use an f-string to show a friendly message. (When the popup appears, type your age!)
challenge.starter:
age = int(input("How old are you? "))  # type your age in the popup
# multiply age by 365 and print a friendly message with an f-string
challenge.solution:
age = int(input("How old are you? "))
days = age * 365
print(f"Wow, you are about {days} days old! 🎉")
challenge.hint: First do age = int(input("...")), then make days = age * 365, then print an f-string using {days}.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Incredible! Your program asks a question, does math, and answers back. That's Day 5 done — you're building real interactive programs now!
